channel.title: Highlights feed
channel.description: Riley J. Shaw is a programmer and designer. Rooted in repair and craft, he creates transparent, remixable tools for a sustainable and accessible future.

item.title: Feature: add a dark theme
item.description:
Dark themes are something I’ve sought since my early days on a computer. I remember finding a way to apply a black background to Microsoft Word 2003, then being horrified when I accidentally printed the document. In uni I scripted my desktop to boot into f.lux’s darkroom mode. One of my only lasting contributions to Signal was loudly advocating for dark mode on iOS and Desktop, not just Android. I even made a browser extension in 2015 to apply a dark theme to the entire Internet.

It’s not the most pressing or exciting work, but I decided my site should have a dark theme of its own. I took a lot of inspiration from my former Khan Academy teammate Josh W. Comeau’s dark mode post, with a few differences.

item.title: Feature: add an embed blocker for external media 🔐
item.description: Makes page loads snappier, and lets folks opt into Google’s tracking empire

item.title: Release: remove the “beta” banner!!! 🎊
item.description:
Almost one year ago, I quietly launched the new version of my site to rileyjshaw.com. It was the last day of the decade, and it felt momentous. But since there were still some glaring bugs and omissions, I decided to hedge the release behind a “THIS SITE IS IN BETA” banner.

It’s the end of the year again. Though there are still design problems and omissions, the site is much further along than it was on initial release. Some highlights:

  • /lab no-longer crashes Webkit based browsers (lol)
  • I added bunch of new scrapers. Most recently: SoundCloud, Vimeo, and YouTube.
  • The design is still uh… getting there… but it’s at least simpler and more consistent now.
  • I test-drove the blog and added some new features (like MDX!)
  • I put a ton of meat on last year’s skelly bones. From /about to /lab to adding about 3x more quotes, this site is more substantial and chaotic than ever before.

item.title: Feature: add a video scraper
item.description: I figure now that I’m scraping SoundCloud, I’d better start scraping YouTube and Vimeo too. Since I occasionally cross-post videos (eg. a demo / instructional video for Patchstorage), I need to figure out how to reduce the clutter caused by duplicate entries. I’ll solve that problem once I see where duplicates tend to crop up.

item.title: Feature: add a SoundCloud song scraper
item.description: For now it’s mostly experiments and technical demos, but as I get braver I might start posting more music :)

item.title: Feature: add lazy loading to the lab
item.description: This small, very important change has been in my working directory since January. What a year…

item.title: Scaling faders (audio demo)
item.description:
I dread mixing audio. I tend to make the drums a little louder, then the lead, then the bass, then the drums again, and on and on until I’m clipping everywhere.

I made this demo UI to show how a mixer might help keep your levels in check. Does this exist yet?

item.title: Deletion Day
item.description: Deletion Day challenges the prevailing notion that “more is more”. In a culture that strives for permanence, we celebrate ephemerality, growth, and change.

item.title: PO-33 loader
item.description: A drum sample loader for my Pocket Operator K.O.

item.title: Plop
item.description: A scriptable text expansion microtool.

item.title: Add pagination to the blog!
item.description:
This is an exciting commit for the new site. It collects blog posts from multiple sources (for now: http://rileyjshaw.commit--blog.com, https://sfpc.rileyjshaw.com, and https://rileyjshaw.com/blog), orders them by date, and paginates them into a nice on-site list.

In the past, my blog has always seemed stale and outdated. By allowing it to gobble content from across the web, I can point it to whatever platform I'm currently publishing on!

This commit is going to show up as a post on my blog, which feels rather meta.

item.title: Bootstrap an independent data scraper
item.description:
Project scraper

The projects on my site are automatically scraped and formatted at publish time using the scripts in this directory. Read more about my reasoning below, or skip to the directory structure.

Why?

Gatsby's source and transformer plugins are powerful, and I used them in the initial development of this site. I eventually decided that separating my collection process would be good for flexibility, control, and offline work.

Flexibility

GraphQL's filters and transforms are powerful, and Gatsby's APIs add more options for how data is fetched, cached, and transformed. However, complicated or non-standard data transforms and sanitization are much easier outside of Gatsby's ecosystem. For instance, the API starts to feel clunky for one-off treatment of specific content nodes,

item.title: Firehose: proof of concept
item.description:
I'm experimenting with auto-generating nodes for https://rileyjshaw.com/lab from a variety of data sources. This project may eventually replace https://rileyjshaw.com.

This is the initial commit, completed quickly as a proof of concept. There's nothing much to show, but I want to deploy ASAP so I can test the full pipeline.

So far, everything has worked! Data from a variety of sources is already appearing on my local server. To reproduce:

  • Run gatsby develop.
  • Open http://localhost:8000/page-2 in a browser.

So far, I'm surfacing data from:

  • A hand-curated JSON list of projects I use for https://rileyjshaw.com/lab.
  • Dwitter's API.
  • My commit--blog's Atom feed.

Setting this up was EASY, which makes me excited for the future of this experiment :)

item.title: Servo
item.description: A fork of the official Arduino Servo library, improved to match standard R/C pulse widths.

item.title: typing lazy (desktop only)
item.description:
Try touch-typing an English word without moving your fingers from their resting position on the keyboard.

This was a proof-of-concept for a keyboard glove, which was implemented in hardware by mapping the trie to a directory tree.

item.title: Huertbeat
item.description: Pulse available Philips hue lights to whatever is playing on Spotify.

item.title: canvas
item.description:
THIRTY-EIGHT rough visual experiments in alphabetical order.

Each experiment was built using a web tool I created in the spirit of OpenFrameworks and TouchDesigner. I wanted libraries close at hand with a simple API for drawing to <canvas>, SVG, etc.

item.title: write
item.description: A chill place to write things.

item.title: Crypto Christmas
item.description:
Improve your online privacy and security over the 12 days of Cryptmas.

I was interviewed for this work by NPR, the CBC, and PCMag.

item.title: RileyJShaw.???
item.description: A single program that outputs my name when you run it through three separate programming languages.

item.title: Cyber pilot (with controls)
item.description: Play this one in a small window. Arrow keys to move around.

item.title: w h a t
item.description: Short narrative experiment written for the PICO-8 fantasy console.

item.title: Virtuoso
item.description:
Play a .mid file at your own speed using a MIDI keyboard!

Works best with MIDI keyboards, but you can use your computer keyboard too.

item.title: Keyboard keyboard
item.description:
Use a MIDI keyboard as your laptop keyboard.

Hard press = UPPERCASE, soft press = lowercase.

item.title: Typing for two
item.description: A competitive typing tutor. Two players type different paragraphs on the same keyboard, simultaneously.

item.title: Single line tiling
item.description:
Tiling patterns from a single spiral with a fixed modulation signal.

Though the line’s formula stays consistent as it spirals out, the tile grid doesn’t increase in size.

item.title: Literate theme
item.description: A CLI tool to update your favorite text editor theme. Emphasizes comments and mutes everything else.

item.title: Circling circles
item.description:
Very simple rule applied to a bunch of particles for a smooth ripple effect.

Changing the timing function on this one is extremely satisfying.

item.title: Jittery visualizer v2
item.description: Added a linear ramp and smoothing function to v1 to make the lowest frequency bin match up with the highest frequency bin in both position and derivative. The effect is a continuous circle, versus the “speech-bubble” notch effect of v1.

item.title: Typelab
item.description: Repeatedly apply a set of transformations to text.

item.title: Astronomie & Astrophysique
item.description: Small canvas demo using Lissajous curves.

item.title: Mechanical waves
item.description:
Possible header for “Mechanical waves and sound” for Khan Academy’s new content redesign.

Includes a few variations.

item.title: whoami.py
item.description:
My contribution to a collection of poems.

From the project page: “there is confusion in the mixed layers of metaphors. This is not an obfuscated code repository; not really. In some ways, it is the opposite.”

item.title: Dark Theme Everywhere
item.description: A forcefully applied dark theme for the web.

item.title: XOXO Bingo
item.description:
Bingo cards generated from the attendance list of XOXOFest. The first person to meet enough attendees “won” the party.

Built hastily between speakers on the last day of the festival; requires login with Twitter to protect attendees’ identity.

item.title: Touchtone math facts
item.description:
A math facts app that you can play from a payphone.

Used to live at the epic phone numbers +1.480.360.MATH (US) and +1.647.360.MATH (Canada).

item.title: Shepard-Risset Glissando
item.description:
Auditory illusion of a tone that continually descends in pitch. It has been described as a “sonic barber’s pole”.

Implemented with the Web Audio API using custom oscillators.

item.title: Challenger
item.description: Pop-up programming challenges in your browser.

item.title: 28 Hour Day
item.description: A Pebble watchface that divides the week into six 28-hour days instead of the standard 24/7.

item.title: Hackers.cool
item.description:
Retro command line emulator using my TinyTerm library.

Type “help” for command list.

item.title: Fingerprint
item.description: In-browser keystroke dynamics demo using Tappy.

item.title: Tappy
item.description: A JavaScript library for recording and comparing rhythms

item.title: Folding tutorial concept
item.description: UI experiment for line-by-line tutorials. Annotations can be turned off to expose a complete code example.

item.title: Tic Attack Toe
item.description:
Rapidfire, turnless tic-tac-toe with a custom websocket lobby.

Inspired by Kung-Fu Chess.

item.title: Reaction
item.description:
Reaction game that steals your history*. It exploits alpha-rounding to create an n-input OR gate for CSS :visited states, then probes links in sequential binary trees.

*This is a proof-of-concept and won’t actually steal anything.

item.title: Tappy game
item.description: Tap the rhythm of the lyrics displayed.

item.title: Film-grain effect in 117B of JavaScript
item.description: Warning: contains a blocking loop that may cause browsers to hang briefly.

item.title: Super slinky
item.description:
No jQuery dependency, section progress shown in headers, click to scroll, resizable with scrollbar on window. Inspired by Slinky.js and DesignWall.

Currently doesn’t work on iOS < 8 since they mess with scroll events. If you use this in production, look into iScroll.

item.title: Pairjam
item.description: Realtime web-based collaborative coding.

item.title: Placid tab
item.description: A minimal New Tab page for Chrome

item.title: Linescapes
item.description: An audiovisual experiment that generates landscapes from sound waves.

item.title: Graphing depth
item.description:
Pretty images from math.

Supports my blog post of the same name.

item.title: Sweep
item.description: A JavaScript library for smoother color transitions

item.title: Own this website
item.description: An experimental online game of King of the Hill, on this very website.

item.title: Tiny boxes
item.description: Chat through happenstance. The interface adapts to meet demand.

item.title: terra
item.description: A JavaScript library for simple biological simulations and cellular automata.

item.title: CSS slope illusion in <140 characters
item.description: Are the base lines straight or at an angle?

item.title: Planets
item.description: Pure Sass animated solar system.

item.title: 2p Snake
item.description: Snake for 2 players on the same keyboard?! Saucy.

item.title: Sass animation grid
item.description: Collection of Sass animations.

item.title: Long-shadow input series
item.description: An attempt to give flat buttons some depth. Collection of inputs including radio buttons, checkboxes, and text inputs.

item.title: Worst practices
item.description:
Some Javascript that I threw together for a friend to 1) solve his problem, 2) explain some hacky JS tricks, and 3) attempt to show him that they aren’t always great to use since they obfuscate your code.

There’s actually some cool stuff going on in here, so I’ve commented it thoroughly.

item.title: Zenwall
item.description:
A depth-sensing grafitti wall for touch-screens & pen tablets.

Demo built for web browsers and Wacom tablets. Installation involved creating a large infrared plane behind a flexible display and sensing deformations using Community Core Vision.
